Party_Details.Name: Danial Patrick Higgins

Facts.Text: By 2010, Danial Patrick Higgins was working as the Managing Director of Montreal Asset Management, a company involved in shipping and oil trading and also providing aviation consultancy services.
On 2010-09-07, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was incorporated with Danial Patrick Higgins, his wife, Nicholas Johnstone, and Stefan Wood as founding directors.
From its incorporation on 2010-09-07, Danial Patrick Higgins held the position of Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.
Around 2001, Nicholas Johnstone first met Danial Patrick Higgins, who was also a pilot with experience in the corporate aviation industry.

On 2010-11-26, at a networking event at the Fullerton Hotel, Danial Patrick Higgins met Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, Chief Executive Officer of InterOil Corporation, and discussed the possibility that Mulacek might purchase a pre-owned corporate jet for business travel.

On 2010-11-29, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Philippe Emanuel Mulacek explaining that a Gulfstream aircraft might suit Mulacek's needs, outlining purchasing options, and stating that Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd could assist with air chartering services, signing off as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter.

During December 2010 and January 2011, Danial Patrick Higgins sent several emails from his Singapore Air Charter email account to Philippe Emanuel Mulacek detailing specifications and prices of various Gulfstream aircraft available for sale, identifying himself as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter and copying Nicholas Johnstone.

On 2011-03-08, AirLNG Ltd was incorporated in Labuan, Malaysia, and was intended to hold beneficial ownership of a Gulfstream III corporate jet to be acquired; Danial Patrick Higgins was involved in arranging the purchase.

On 2011-03-23, a Letter of Intent for the purchase of a Gulfstream III aircraft was signed between AirLNG Ltd and OK Consultants Ltd after draft terms had been amended by Danial Patrick Higgins, with related emails being sent to his Montreal Asset Management email account.

On 2011-04-25, an Aircraft Management Services Agreement was concluded between Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd and AirLNG Ltd, under which Singapore Air Charter would manage, operate, and maintain the Gulfstream III aircraft for AirLNG's exclusive use.

Under the Aircraft Management Services Agreement dated 2011-04-25, AirLNG Ltd was to pay Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd a monthly management fee of US$25,000 and US$11,875 per month for each primary flight crew member.

On 2011-04-26, Henry Edmond Aldorf granted a power of attorney to Danial Patrick Higgins authorising him to act on behalf of AirLNG Ltd and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd in relation to the purchase of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

On 2011-05-17, instructions were issued by Danial Patrick Higgins on behalf of AirLNG Ltd and by Jack Kendall on behalf of OK Consultants Ltd for disbursement of approximately US$2.3 million held in escrow for the aircraft purchase, including US$1.35 million as purchase price and several reimbursement payments.
On the completion date 2011-05-18, US$1.35 million from the escrow account was to be transferred to an account specified by OK Consultants Ltd as the purchase price of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

On 2011-05-18, the escrow disbursement instructions provided for US$15,000 to be transferred to Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, US$316,500 to Montreal Asset Management, US$316,500 to OK Consultants Ltd, US$1,690 as escrow fees, US$4,400 to Danial Patrick Higgins for pre-buy inspection instructions, and US$2,325 to OK Consultants Ltd.

From around early 2011, Danial Patrick Higgins acted as the primary point of contact between Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd and the owners of the Gulfstream III aircraft for arranging charter flights, with Nicholas Johnstone and Danny Walker serving as primary pilots.

Around August 2011, Danial Patrick Higgins participated in discussions with the other directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd about a proposal from Philippe Emanuel Mulacek for Singapore Air Charter to assist in running a Papua New Guinea aircraft management company called National Air Services.

On 2011-09-20, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Philippe Emanuel Mulacek stating that the US Federal Aviation Administration had introduced rules affecting ownership and registration of US-registered aircraft and that an alternative jurisdiction for registration of the Gulfstream III should be found.

On 2012-01-25, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed the other directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd stating that he had recently met Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, that they were unhappy with issues including Nicholas Johnstone's resignation as pilot and Danny Walker's extended vacation, and that two possible courses of action had been discussed: an investment by Mulacek into Singapore Air Charter or termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and reassignment of the contract to a new company to be headed by Higgins.

On 2012-01-27, a meeting took place at InterOil Corporation's Singapore office involving Danial Patrick Higgins, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, during which they discussed matters including the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and future management of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

At the end of December 2011, Nicholas Johnstone resigned from his position as a pilot of the Gulfstream III aircraft on health grounds but indicated willingness to continue flying until May 2012 if necessary.

Around late December 2011, Danny Walker returned to Australia for a four-week vacation over the Christmas and New Year period while serving as manager of flight operations for Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.
By late 2011, the Gulfstream III aircraft was grounded for scheduled maintenance and remained out of service longer than expected due to corrosion detected at the wing.
On 2012-02-13, the Gulfstream III aircraft returned to service following an extended maintenance period.

By early 2012, relations between Danial Patrick Higgins and fellow directors Danny Walker and Stefan Wood at Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd had deteriorated to the point where Higgins was no longer on speaking terms with them, with Nicholas Johnstone acting as intermediary.

On 2012-01-29, Danny Walker tendered his resignation as manager of flight operations at Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd but offered to continue serving as a pilot; Danial Patrick Higgins accepted the resignation from the managerial role and declined the offer to continue as pilot.

On 2012-02-09, Benjamin Ng of Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd sent a draft notice of termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement to Danial Patrick Higgins at Higgins's personal email address.

On 2012-02-10, Danial Patrick Higgins met Benjamin Ng, received a printed copy of the termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, and signed it on behalf of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.

On 2012-02-28, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed his fellow directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd an electronic copy of the draft termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement that he had originally received on 2012-02-09.
Under the terms of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, the termination notice given in February 2012 resulted in the agreement ending with effect from 2012-03-22.

Between 2012-02-10 and 2012-02-28, after signing the termination notice, Danial Patrick Higgins did not inform the other directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd that he had signed the notice on behalf of the company.

On 2012-03-12, Jack Kendall of OK Consultants Ltd emailed Danial Patrick Higgins stating that his company was under pressure from US tax authorities regarding the Gulfstream III aircraft and insisting that the aircraft be transferred to another registry or registered under a different company if it remained on the US registry.

On 2012-03-12, after receiving Jack Kendall's email, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Philippe Emanuel Mulacek asking for instructions on which country's registry should be arranged on an interim basis for the Gulfstream III aircraft.
On 2012-03-16, the Gulfstream III aircraft was flown to Malaysia to be parked while efforts were made to register it in Malaysia.

By 2012-03-19, efforts were underway to register the Gulfstream III aircraft in Malaysia, but it was later discovered that such registration would be prohibitively expensive and time-consuming because it would be the first of its type in that jurisdiction.

On 2012-04-14, a letter on AirLNG Ltd letterhead signed by Philippe Emanuel Mulacek was issued to the Malaysian Department of Civil Aviation stating that Danial Patrick Higgins was a consultant to AirLNG Ltd and authorised to approve and sign all documents relating to the company's aircraft and its certification and registration in Malaysia.
On 2012-03-28, Danial Patrick Higgins incorporated Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd, a company intended to provide aircraft management services.

On 2012-03-29, Danial Patrick Higgins sent a draft aircraft management contract on behalf of Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd to Benjamin Ng with instructions that it be forwarded to Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, and Henry Edmond Aldorf; the draft terms were substantially similar to the earlier Aircraft Management Services Agreement.

On 2012-04-10, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Benjamin Ng stating that he was doing significant work to get the Gulfstream III aircraft re-registered essentially without payment at that time, and that he had paid salaries of contract pilots, suggesting that any new contract should be backdated when signed.

On 2012-04-15, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Philippe Emanuel Mulacek requesting a short-term consultancy agreement with AirLNG Ltd backdated to 2012-03-22 at US$35,000 per month plus expenses, and also requesting a letter of authority to allow him to sign documents for Malaysian registration of the aircraft.

On 2012-04-17, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed Benjamin Ng stating that he needed a decision on whether AirLNG Ltd would fund a Gulfstream III simulator training course for a new captain, and that without such a decision he would remove the pilot from the course.
On 2012-04-30, Danial Patrick Higgins sent an invoice for US$22,000 to Benjamin Ng for a Gulfstream III simulator training course for a pilot.
On 2012-04-30, Benjamin Ng replied to Danial Patrick Higgins stating that AirLNG Ltd had not committed to a new management agreement and had not committed to hiring and training a new pilot.

On 2012-05-01, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek emailed Danial Patrick Higgins stating that items relating to the Gulfstream III aircraft should be placed on hold, that proposed contract costs were too high, and that core management of the aircraft would be taken over by others.

On 2012-05-01, after receiving instructions from Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, Danial Patrick Higgins indicated that he would place further work on the Gulfstream III aircraft on hold pending a decision on how to proceed.
At the start of May 2012, Danial Patrick Higgins was asked by representatives of AirLNG Ltd to stop work relating to registration of the Gulfstream III aircraft, and he ceased such work.
On 2012-05-08, AirLNG Ltd entered into an aircraft management contract with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd for management of the Gulfstream III aircraft.
On 2012-07-11, the Gulfstream III aircraft was re-registered on the United States aircraft register under arrangements made by Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd.
On 2012-07-31, the Gulfstream III aircraft received a certificate of airworthiness following its re-registration in the United States and then returned to active service.
On 2012-06-22, Stefan Wood requisitioned an extraordinary general meeting of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd to consider removal of Danial Patrick Higgins and his wife as directors.

On 2012-07-19, an extraordinary general meeting of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was convened at which resolutions were passed to remove Danial Patrick Higgins and his wife as directors, and their tendered resignations were recorded in the minutes.

On 2012-07-19, before commencement of the extraordinary general meeting of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, Danial Patrick Higgins and his wife attended to tender their resignations as directors and left immediately afterwards.

In April 2012, AirLNG Ltd arranged charter flights using Elite Jets Sdn Bhd for routes including Makassar and Ambon at a quoted cost of US$116,000, while AirLNG Ltd was informed by Danial Patrick Higgins that the charters would cost US$140,000.

In April 2012, AirLNG Ltd transferred US$90,000 directly to Elite Jets Sdn Bhd for Makassar-Ambon charter flights, and Montreal Asset Management transferred US$26,000 to Elite Jets Sdn Bhd, with AirLNG Ltd later transferring US$50,000 to Montreal Asset Management at the request of Danial Patrick Higgins.

Between 2012-02-13 and 2012-03-22, after the Gulfstream III aircraft returned to service and before the effective termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, Danial Patrick Higgins continued to manage charter operations for AirLNG Ltd through Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.

Between 2012-03-23 and early May 2012, after the Aircraft Management Services Agreement ended, Danial Patrick Higgins continued to perform work relating to the Gulfstream III aircraft, including efforts toward registration and arranging pilots, without a new written management contract in place.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and communications, Danial Patrick Higgins had any binding entitlement to remuneration from Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, or AirLNG Ltd for work done in relation to the Gulfstream III aircraft after termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, and whether his actions as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd contributed to the loss of that agreement and to any disputed financial gains from aircraft-related transactions.
Party_Details.Rule: A binding contract requires a clear agreement on essential terms between identified parties. Where services are performed without an express contract, payment may arise only if a contract can be implied from the parties' conduct or if the recipient has been unjustly enriched at the provider's expense under recognised unjust factors. Company directors and managing directors must act in the best interests of their company, avoid conflicts between personal interests and company interests, and must not obtain undisclosed profits or divert corporate opportunities that arise in connection with their office.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute emails and meetings Danial Patrick Higgins promoted Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd as service provider, arranged the purchase and management of the Gulfstream III aircraft, and later discussed alternative structures with Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli when dissatisfaction with Singapore Air Charter's performance arose. The communications in early 2012 indicate ongoing negotiations about future management arrangements and consultancy terms, including draft contracts and requests for backdated consultancy agreements, but they also AirLNG Ltd did not confirm a new management agreement or commit to pilot training costs. Higgins incorporated Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd while still serving as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter and sent a draft management contract for that new company to take over the aircraft management business. He signed the termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement on 2012-02-10 without promptly informing his fellow directors and later forwarded only the draft version to them. Financial instructions for the aircraft purchase and later charter flights show specific payments to Singapore Air Charter, Montreal Asset Management, and others, including a margin between the amount quoted to AirLNG Ltd for Makassar-Ambon charters and the cost charged by Elite Jets Sdn Bhd. These pre-dispute events frame the later disputes over whether Higgins had any contractual or restitutionary right to further remuneration and whether his conduct as managing director aligned with the interests of Singapore Air Charter or positioned his own entities to replace its role.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court analysed whether any oral contract existed on 27 January 2012 or 8 May 2012 between Higgins and the defendants, concluding that there was no consensus on essential terms such as remuneration and that contemporaneous correspondence showed only negotiations and expectations rather than a concluded agreement. The judge noted that AirLNG Ltd, as the special purpose vehicle used for tax reasons and as the party to both the AMS Agreement and the later AAC agreement, would logically have been the contracting party if any new management contract had been formed, which undermined Higgins's pleaded case against the individual defendants and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd. The court found that Higgins's own emails, including the 29 March 2012 draft SAM management contract and the 10 April and 15 April 2012 messages, demonstrated that he was seeking to secure a new contract rather than documenting an existing oral agreement. The judge placed weight on Higgins's failure to mention any oral contract in contemporaneous communications and on the late change in his pleaded date and narrative of the alleged agreement, which undermined his credibility. On the restitutionary claim, the court applied unjust enrichment principles, holding that Higgins could not recover for pre-termination flights he had withdrawn, for Makassar-Ambon charters that had already been fully paid and yielded a profit to his company, or for pilot training that he arranged without clear authorisation after warning that he would cancel if no decision was made. For aircraft registration work, the court held that Higgins's advice to deregister from the US and pursue Malaysian registration was based on incorrect assumptions and that he had not made preliminary checks before moving the aircraft, so the work did not confer an objective benefit and any enrichment was not unjust, particularly as he had taken the risk of working in anticipation of a contract that never materialised. Suit 244 was therefore dismissed in full. In Suit 733, the court accepted the evidence of Mulacek and Civelli that Higgins had described Singapore Air Charter as dysfunctional and proposed that the AMS Agreement be terminated and replaced by a new company he would control. The judge found that Higgins's conduct in signing the termination notice secretly, delaying disclosure to his co-directors, and incorporating Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd while still managing director of Singapore Air Charter placed him in a position of conflict and showed that he was not acting in Singapore Air Charter's best interests. Applying the Brickenden approach to causation for core fiduciary breaches, the court held that Higgins's conduct was causally linked to the loss of the AMS Agreement and that he had not shown that the agreement would have ended in any event, rejecting arguments based on alleged contributory fault of other directors. The court determined that Singapore Air Charter was entitled to equitable compensation for lost profits over the remaining contractual term of the AMS Agreement, with the precise quantum to be assessed by the Registrar because the evidence did not allow a reliable deduction for operating expenses. On secret profits, the court examined the escrow disbursement and Makassar-Ambon charter flows, concluding that Higgins, through Montreal Asset Management, had obtained undisclosed financial benefits connected with corporate opportunities arising from his role, and that such profits were recoverable by Singapore Air Charter. Overall, Suit 733 was allowed in favour of Singapore Air Charter on its main claims, with Higgins's counterclaim for unpaid salary allowed only in part.

Party_Details.Name: Philippe Emanuel Mulacek

Facts.Text: By 2010, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek was the Chief Executive Officer of InterOil Corporation, a company publicly listed in the United States.

On 2010-11-26, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek met Danial Patrick Higgins at a networking event at the Fullerton Hotel in Singapore and discussed the possibility of purchasing a pre-owned corporate jet for business travel.

In March 2011, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek was involved in discussions with Carlo Giuseppe Civelli and Danial Patrick Higgins that led to the decision to purchase a Gulfstream III corporate jet through AirLNG Ltd as a special purpose vehicle.

On 2011-03-08, AirLNG Ltd was incorporated in Labuan, Malaysia, with the intention that it would beneficially hold the Gulfstream III aircraft used for business travel by Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and associates.

On 2011-03-23, a Letter of Intent for the purchase of the Gulfstream III aircraft was signed on behalf of AirLNG Ltd and OK Consultants Ltd following negotiations in which Philippe Emanuel Mulacek was one of the decision-makers.

On 2011-04-25, AirLNG Ltd, associated with Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, entered into an Aircraft Management Services Agreement with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd for management of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

From mid-2011, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek regularly used the Gulfstream III aircraft managed by Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd for travel to Papua New Guinea and other destinations in connection with business activities.
Around August 2011, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek proposed that Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd assist with running a Papua New Guinea aircraft management company called National Air Services.

On 2011-09-21, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek replied to an email from Danial Patrick Higgins acknowledging information about new US Federal Aviation Administration rules affecting ownership and registration of US-registered aircraft.

By late 2011, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek expressed dissatisfaction with the extended downtime of the Gulfstream III aircraft during maintenance and with operational issues involving Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.

On 2012-01-27, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek attended a meeting at InterOil Corporation's Singapore office with Danial Patrick Higgins and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli to discuss the future management of the Gulfstream III aircraft and the status of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement.

On 2012-02-09, through his financial controller Benjamin Ng, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek caused a draft notice of termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement to be sent to Danial Patrick Higgins.

On 2012-02-10, after Danial Patrick Higgins signed the termination notice, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, through AirLNG Ltd, proceeded on the basis that the Aircraft Management Services Agreement with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd would end in accordance with its notice provisions.

On 2012-03-12, after receiving an email from Jack Kendall about US tax authority pressure regarding the Gulfstream III aircraft, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek received a proposal from Danial Patrick Higgins to move the aircraft to another registry and approved a plan to fly the aircraft to Malaysia and park it pending re-registration.
On 2012-03-16, following approval from Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, the Gulfstream III aircraft was flown to Malaysia to be parked while registration options were explored.

On 2012-04-14, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek signed a letter on AirLNG Ltd letterhead addressed to the Malaysian Department of Civil Aviation authorising Danial Patrick Higgins to represent AirLNG Ltd and to sign all documents relating to certification and registration of the Gulfstream III aircraft in Malaysia.

On 2012-05-01, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek emailed Danial Patrick Higgins stating that contracts for aircraft management were too costly, that items relating to the Gulfstream III should be placed on hold, and that core management would be taken over by others.
On 2012-05-08, AirLNG Ltd, associated with Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, signed an aircraft management contract with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd to manage the Gulfstream III aircraft.
On 2012-07-11, under arrangements made by Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd for AirLNG Ltd, the Gulfstream III aircraft was re-registered on the United States aircraft register.

On 2012-07-31, the Gulfstream III aircraft obtained a certificate of airworthiness following its re-registration in the United States and resumed active service for business use by AirLNG Ltd and its stakeholders including Philippe Emanuel Mulacek.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute dealings, Philippe Emanuel Mulacek or entities associated with him had any contractual or restitutionary obligation to remunerate Danial Patrick Higgins for post-termination work on the Gulfstream III aircraft, and whether decisions taken with Higgins regarding termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and subsequent engagement of a new manager affected Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd's interests.
Party_Details.Rule: A person or company becomes bound to pay for services only where a contract is formed on clear terms or where the law of unjust enrichment requires restitution for benefits received at another's expense in circumstances recognised as unjust. When dealing with a company managed by a director, counterparties may rely on that director as point of contact, but internal duties between the director and the company are governed by company law and do not automatically impose liability on the counterparty.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute record shows that Philippe Emanuel Mulacek engaged with Danial Patrick Higgins initially through Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd for acquisition and management of the Gulfstream III aircraft, using AirLNG Ltd as the contracting vehicle. Dissatisfaction with aircraft downtime and operational issues led to discussions in January 2012 about alternatives, after which a termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement was prepared and signed. Mulacek, through AirLNG Ltd and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, used Higgins as the operational contact but did not sign any new written management or consultancy agreement with Higgins or his new company after termination. Instead, AirLNG Ltd contracted with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd on 2012-05-08. Payments for the aircraft purchase and later charters were made according to specific instructions, including sums to Singapore Air Charter, Montreal Asset Management, and OK Consultants Ltd. These facts frame the later dispute over whether Mulacek or his associated entities owed Higgins further remuneration and whether their acceptance of Higgins's proposals contributed to Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd losing its only management contract.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that no oral contract was formed between Higgins and Mulacek (or Civelli or Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd) on either 27 January 2012 or 8 May 2012. It reasoned that AirLNG Ltd, not the individuals or Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, was the logical contracting party for aircraft management, and that contemporaneous emails showed ongoing negotiations rather than a concluded agreement. The judge found that Mulacek's authorisation of Higgins to act for AirLNG Ltd in Malaysian registration and his willingness to consider future cooperation did not amount to a binding commitment to pay Higgins US$25,000 or US$35,000 per month. On unjust enrichment, the court concluded that AirLNG Ltd and related parties had already paid for flights and that Higgins's additional work on registration and pilot training did not confer an objective benefit or was undertaken at Higgins's own risk in anticipation of a contract. Accordingly, the claims in Suit 244 against Mulacek and the other defendants were dismissed. In Suit 733, the court accepted Mulacek's evidence that Higgins had described Singapore Air Charter as dysfunctional and proposed that the Aircraft Management Services Agreement be terminated and replaced by a new company controlled by Higgins. The judge found that Mulacek and Civelli had relied on Higgins as their only point of contact at Singapore Air Charter and that their decision to terminate was influenced by Higgins's representations. However, the court did not impose liability on Mulacek in Suit 733; instead, it focused on Higgins's fiduciary breaches toward Singapore Air Charter. Mulacek's role was relevant as context for causation and for assessing Higgins's conduct, but not as a basis for liability to Higgins.

Party_Details.Name: Carlo Giuseppe Civelli

Facts.Text: By 2011, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli was an investor in InterOil Corporation and the beneficial owner of Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd.
On 2011-03-08, AirLNG Ltd was incorporated in Labuan, Malaysia, with Carlo Giuseppe Civelli and Henry Edmond Aldorf as founding directors, to hold beneficial ownership of a Gulfstream III aircraft.
By May 2011, approximately US$2.3 million had been deposited into an escrow account by Carlo Giuseppe Civelli to fund the purchase and related expenses of the Gulfstream III aircraft for AirLNG Ltd.

On 2011-03-23, following discussions with Danial Patrick Higgins and Philippe Emanuel Mulacek, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli agreed to proceed with the purchase of the Gulfstream III aircraft from OK Consultants Ltd through AirLNG Ltd.

On 2011-04-25, AirLNG Ltd, directed by Carlo Giuseppe Civelli and Henry Edmond Aldorf, entered into an Aircraft Management Services Agreement with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd for management of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

From mid-2011, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli used the Gulfstream III aircraft managed by Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd for travel to Papua New Guinea and other destinations in connection with business interests.

On 2012-01-27, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli attended a meeting at InterOil Corporation's Singapore office with Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and Danial Patrick Higgins to discuss the status of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and future management of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

On 2012-02-10, after Danial Patrick Higgins signed the termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, through his role in AirLNG Ltd and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, proceeded on the basis that the agreement with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd would end in accordance with its terms.
On 2012-05-08, AirLNG Ltd, associated with Carlo Giuseppe Civelli, entered into an aircraft management contract with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd to manage the Gulfstream III aircraft.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute arrangements and payments, Carlo Giuseppe Civelli or entities associated with him had any obligation to remunerate Danial Patrick Higgins beyond amounts already paid for the aircraft purchase and management, and how his role in funding and directing AirLNG Ltd related to the termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Rule: Individuals who fund and direct a special purpose vehicle are generally bound through that vehicle's contracts rather than personally, unless they separately agree to personal obligations. Payments made through escrow and management agreements are governed by the terms of those instruments, and additional obligations arise only if new contracts are formed or if unjust enrichment principles apply.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute facts Carlo Giuseppe Civelli funded the escrow for the Gulfstream III purchase, served as a founding director of AirLNG Ltd, and beneficially owned Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd. AirLNG Ltd contracted with Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd under the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and later with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd. The escrow instructions allocated specific sums to Singapore Air Charter, Montreal Asset Management, and OK Consultants Ltd, reflecting agreed compensation structures at the time of purchase. There is no pre-dispute record of a separate personal remuneration agreement between Civelli and Higgins outside the corporate structures. Decisions about terminating the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and appointing a new manager were taken through AirLNG Ltd, with Higgins as operational contact, and form the factual background to later disputes about Higgins's expectations and Singapore Air Charter's loss of business.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated AirLNG Ltd as the proper contracting party for aircraft management and acquisition, noting that Civelli's role as funder and director did not by itself create personal contractual obligations to Higgins. In assessing the alleged oral contracts, the judge found it implausible that experienced businessmen like Civelli and Mulacek would bypass AirLNG Ltd and enter personal agreements that would undermine the tax advantages of the special purpose vehicle. The court concluded that no oral contract was formed on 27 January 2012 or 8 May 2012 and that Higgins's restitutionary claims failed for lack of benefit or unjust factor. In Suit 733, Civelli's evidence that Higgins had described Singapore Air Charter as dysfunctional and proposed termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement was accepted as credible and consistent with contemporaneous documents. However, liability in Suit 733 was imposed on Higgins for fiduciary breaches toward Singapore Air Charter, not on Civelli. Civelli's involvement was relevant to understanding the commercial context and causation but did not result in findings of liability against him.

Party_Details.Name: Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: By 2011, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd was a Singapore-incorporated company beneficially owned by Carlo Giuseppe Civelli and involved in business activities related to Papua New Guinea.

On 2011-04-26, Henry Edmond Aldorf granted a power of attorney to Danial Patrick Higgins authorising him to act on behalf of AirLNG Ltd and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd in relation to the purchase of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

From mid-2011, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd personnel, including financial controller Benjamin Ng, assisted Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli with matters relating to the Gulfstream III aircraft.

On 2012-02-09, Benjamin Ng, a financial controller with Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, emailed a draft notice of termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement to Danial Patrick Higgins at Higgins's request.

On 2012-02-10, after Danial Patrick Higgins signed the termination notice, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, through Benjamin Ng, continued to coordinate with Higgins and Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd on transitional matters relating to the Gulfstream III aircraft.

On 2012-04-10, Benjamin Ng received an email from Danial Patrick Higgins stating that Higgins was doing work on re-registration of the Gulfstream III aircraft and had paid salaries of contract pilots, and that any new contract should be backdated when signed.

On 2012-04-16, Benjamin Ng emailed Danial Patrick Higgins suggesting that Higgins discuss a temporary consultancy arrangement with Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and indicating that there might be an understanding about future cooperation.

On 2012-04-30, Benjamin Ng replied to an invoice from Danial Patrick Higgins for a pilot simulator course, stating that AirLNG Ltd had not committed to a new management agreement or to hiring and training a new pilot.

Around early May 2012, Benjamin Ng informed Danial Patrick Higgins that Higgins should stop work on registration of the Gulfstream III aircraft and took steps to engage another aircraft management company for AirLNG Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute authorisations and communications, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd incurred any direct contractual or restitutionary liability to Danial Patrick Higgins for work performed on the Gulfstream III aircraft, and how its coordination role through Benjamin Ng related to the termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and appointment of a new manager.
Party_Details.Rule: A company that authorises an individual to act on its behalf in a transaction is bound by contracts properly formed within that authority, but additional obligations to pay for services arise only where contracts are concluded or where unjust enrichment principles apply. Coordination and communication by a related company do not by themselves create separate payment obligations beyond those of the contracting entity.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute facts Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, through a power of attorney and through its financial controller Benjamin Ng, facilitated the purchase and management of the Gulfstream III aircraft for AirLNG Ltd and its stakeholders. Ng prepared and sent the draft termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement at Higgins's request and later discussed potential consultancy arrangements and expenses with Higgins. However, Ng's emails consistently indicated that no new management agreement or pilot training commitment had been made, and AirLNG Ltd, not Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd, ultimately contracted with Asia Aviation Company Pte Ltd. These facts Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd acted as a coordinating entity rather than as a principal in a separate remuneration agreement with Higgins.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considered Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd primarily as a vehicle through which Civelli and Mulacek managed aspects of the aircraft project, including communications via Benjamin Ng. In assessing Higgins's contractual and restitutionary claims, the judge focused on AirLNG Ltd as the contracting party and found no evidence of a separate binding agreement between Higgins and Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd. The court noted that Ng's emails showed that no new management or consultancy contract had been agreed and that expenses such as pilot training were not authorised. Consequently, the claims in Suit 244 against Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd were dismissed. In Suit 733, Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd's role was relevant to reconstructing the sequence of events leading to termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and to evaluating Higgins's conduct, but no fiduciary or other liability was imposed on Pacific LNG Operations Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Name: Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was incorporated on 2010-09-07 in Singapore to provide air chartering and related aviation services.
On incorporation on 2010-09-07, the directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd included Nicholas Johnstone, Stefan Wood, Danial Patrick Higgins, and the wife of Danial Patrick Higgins.
From 2010-09-07, Danial Patrick Higgins served as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd.

On 2011-04-25, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd entered into an Aircraft Management Services Agreement with AirLNG Ltd under which Singapore Air Charter would manage, operate, and maintain a Gulfstream III aircraft for AirLNG's exclusive use in return for a monthly management fee and pilot payments.

From mid-2011, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd operated the Gulfstream III aircraft for AirLNG Ltd, with Nicholas Johnstone and Danny Walker serving as primary pilots and Danial Patrick Higgins acting as primary point of contact for charter arrangements.

Around August 2011, the directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, including Danial Patrick Higgins, Nicholas Johnstone, and Stefan Wood, discussed a proposal for the company to assist in running a Papua New Guinea aircraft management company called National Air Services, but the proposal was not pursued because none of the directors wished to be based permanently in Papua New Guinea.

By late 2011, disagreements had arisen among the directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd over issues including Danial Patrick Higgins's remuneration, his level of commitment to the company, and proposed salary increases for Nicholas Johnstone and Danny Walker.

By late 2011, the Gulfstream III aircraft managed by Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was grounded for scheduled maintenance and remained out of service longer than expected due to corrosion detected at the wing.
At the end of December 2011, Nicholas Johnstone resigned as a pilot of the Gulfstream III aircraft for health reasons but indicated willingness to continue flying until May 2012 if necessary.
Around late December 2011, Danny Walker, manager of flight operations at Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, left for a four-week vacation in Australia over the Christmas and New Year period.

On 2012-01-25, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed the other directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd stating that Philippe Emanuel Mulacek and Carlo Giuseppe Civelli were unhappy with operational issues and that two options had been discussed: Mulacek investing in Singapore Air Charter or terminating the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and reassigning the contract to a new company headed by Higgins.

On 2012-01-29, Danny Walker resigned as manager of flight operations at Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd but offered to continue as a pilot; his resignation from the managerial role was accepted and his offer to continue as pilot was declined.
On 2012-02-09, a draft notice of termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement addressed to Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was prepared by Benjamin Ng and sent to Danial Patrick Higgins.
On 2012-02-10, Danial Patrick Higgins signed the termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement on behalf of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd in the presence of Benjamin Ng.

Under the terms of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, the termination notice signed on 2012-02-10 resulted in the agreement ending with effect from 2012-03-22, after which Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd ceased to be the contracted manager of the Gulfstream III aircraft.

Between 2012-02-10 and 2012-02-28, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd's other directors were not informed by Danial Patrick Higgins that he had already signed the termination notice for the Aircraft Management Services Agreement.

On 2012-02-28, Danial Patrick Higgins emailed the other directors of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd an electronic copy of the draft termination notice originally sent on 2012-02-09, without disclosing that he had signed a final version on 2012-02-10.

Between 2012-03-23 and 2012-03-31, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd continued to provide limited services in relation to the Gulfstream III aircraft during the notice period following termination of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement.

In April 2012, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, through Danial Patrick Higgins and Montreal Asset Management, arranged charter flights using Elite Jets Sdn Bhd for routes including Makassar and Ambon while the Gulfstream III aircraft was grounded.

In April 2012, for Makassar-Ambon charter flights arranged while the Gulfstream III aircraft was grounded, AirLNG Ltd paid US$90,000 directly to Elite Jets Sdn Bhd and US$50,000 to Montreal Asset Management, while Montreal Asset Management paid US$26,000 to Elite Jets Sdn Bhd, resulting in a US$24,000 surplus retained by Montreal Asset Management.
On 2012-06-22, director Stefan Wood requisitioned an extraordinary general meeting of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd to consider removal of Danial Patrick Higgins and his wife as directors.

On 2012-07-19, an extraordinary general meeting of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd was held at which resolutions were passed to remove Danial Patrick Higgins and his wife as directors, and their resignations were recorded.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on pre-dispute conduct and internal governance, Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd lost the benefit of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and related corporate opportunities as a result of actions taken by its Managing Director, and whether any undisclosed financial gains connected to aircraft transactions should be recoverable by the company.
Party_Details.Rule: A company is entitled to the benefit of its contracts and corporate opportunities. Its directors and managing director must act in the company's best interests, avoid conflicts of interest, and must not divert business or profits arising from their position to themselves or competing entities without informed consent. If a director's conduct causes the company to lose a profitable contract or corporate opportunity, the company may seek equitable compensation for lost profits and recovery of undisclosed gains.
Party_Details.Application: The pre-dispute facts Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd's only significant contract was the Aircraft Management Services Agreement with AirLNG Ltd for the Gulfstream III aircraft. Operational difficulties and internal disagreements arose in late 2011, but the company continued to manage the aircraft. In January 2012, Danial Patrick Higgins informed his co-directors that AirLNG Ltd's principals were unhappy and that options included investment into Singapore Air Charter or termination and reassignment of the contract to a new company he would head. Higgins then signed the termination notice on 2012-02-10 without promptly informing the board and later forwarded only the draft version. Shortly after, he incorporated Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd and sent a draft management contract for that new company to take over the aircraft management business. Financial flows around the Makassar-Ambon charters Montreal Asset Management, controlled by Higgins, retained a US$24,000 surplus on charters arranged while the Gulfstream III aircraft was grounded. These events form the factual basis for Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd's later claims that its managing director's conduct contributed to the loss of its management contract and to undisclosed profits from aircraft-related opportunities.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that Higgins, as Managing Director of Singapore Air Charter Pte Ltd, owed core fiduciary duties including acting in the company's best interests, avoiding conflicts, and not making secret profits. It accepted the evidence of Mulacek and Civelli that Higgins had described Singapore Air Charter as incompetent or dysfunctional and proposed that the Aircraft Management Services Agreement be terminated and replaced by a new company he would control. The judge held that Higgins's conduct in secretly signing the termination notice on 10 February 2012, delaying disclosure to his co-directors, and then incorporating Singapore Aircraft Management Pte Ltd and sending a draft management contract for that new company while still in office placed him in a position of conflict and showed that he was not acting for Singapore Air Charter's benefit. Applying the Brickenden approach, the court concluded that Higgins's breaches were causally linked to the loss of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement and that he had not shown that the contract would have ended in any event. The court determined that Singapore Air Charter was entitled to equitable compensation for lost profits over the remaining contractual term of the Aircraft Management Services Agreement, with the precise quantum to be assessed by the Registrar due to limited evidence on incremental operating expenses. On secret profits, the court held that the US$24,000 surplus retained by Montreal Asset Management from the Makassar-Ambon charters and the back-to-back profit from the aircraft acquisition were corporate opportunities or gains connected to Higgins's position and were recoverable by Singapore Air Charter. Suit 733 was therefore allowed in favour of Singapore Air Charter on its main claims, subject to assessment of quantum, while Higgins's counterclaim for unpaid salary was allowed only in part.
